did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning, specifically an ensemble method combining a modified Inception ResNet v2 with a Soft-Attention mechanism and a modified EfficientNet-B4, to perform skin lesion classification and melanoma diagnosis on the ISIC-2017 and ISIC-2018 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes a new framework for skin lesion classification using an ensemble method combining a modified Inception ResNet v2 and EfficientNet-B4. It details specific modifications (Soft-Attention mechanism, dense layers), describes experimental evaluation on ISIC-2017 and ISIC-2018 datasets, and reports original results including accuracy, precision, recall, and F-measures, distinguishing it from a review paper.

title: Skin Lesion Classification via ensemble method on deep learning
brief_summary: This study proposes a novel ensemble deep learning framework for skin lesion classification and melanoma diagnosis to address challenges in dermoscopy interpretation. The method combines a modified Inception ResNet v2, enhanced with a Soft-Attention mechanism to highlight informative features, and a modified EfficientNet-B4 with added dense layers. Predictions from these two models are aggregated using a soft voting strategy to improve classification robustness. The approach was evaluated on the ISIC-2017 and ISIC-2018 datasets using metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, and F-measures. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed ensemble method achieves higher accuracy than current state-of-the-art techniques, with weighted average accuracies of 79.5% on ISIC-2017 and 85.18% on ISIC-2018.

lesion_types_studied.details: Studied in both ISIC-2017 (117 test samples) and ISIC-2018 (171 test samples) datasets. The paper focuses on melanoma diagnosis and classification.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions (e.g., Melanoma, Nevus, Seborrheic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, etc.)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs Skin Lesion Classification (SLC) and melanoma diagnosis. It uses a modified Inception ResNet v2 and EfficientNet-B4 ensemble with soft voting to predict discrete classes from the ISIC-2017 (3 classes: Melanoma, Seborrheic Keratosis, Nevus) and ISIC-2018 (7 classes: Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevi, Basal cell carcinoma, Actinic keratosis, Benign keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular lesion) datasets. Performance is evaluated using Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-score, Specificity, and AUC.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing 2750 dermoscopy skin lesion images with a size of 767 × 1022 pixels, belonging to three categories. It was used for skin lesion classification and melanoma diagnosis.

use.training: Used for training the ensemble model (modified Inception ResNet v2 and modified EfficientNet-B4) with data augmentation (rotation, shifting, zooming, flipping).
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process.
use.testing: Used for testing and evaluating the performance of the proposed method using metrics like Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-score, and Specificity.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing skin images taken using a dermatoscope, divided into seven categories representing different types of skin diseases. Images were resized to 380 × 380 pixels.

use.training: Used for training the ensemble model with data augmentation.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process.
use.testing: Used for testing and evaluating the performance of the proposed method. Additionally, a subset of 30% of the total samples (3005 images) was reserved for testing to compare with reference [39], while 70% (7010 images) were used for training and validation.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To calculate performance metrics including Accuracy, Precision, Recall (Sensitivity), F1-score, and Specificity for evaluating the classification results on ISIC-2017 and ISIC-2018 datasets.

statistical_methods_used.method: Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) Curve and Area Under the Curve (AUC)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the model's ability to distinguish between positive and negative instances at various threshold settings and to provide a comprehensive metric for overall performance.
To aggregate predictions from the modified Inception ResNet v2 and modified EfficientNet-B4 models by averaging their probability distributions for final classification.
To average the performance indices (metrics) by calculating weights from the support samples, addressing class imbalance in the datasets.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-score, Specificity, and AUC, and compared qualitatively to state-of-the-art methods.

ISIC-2017.notes: Class weights were estimated from support samples to average the indices. The ensemble method achieved higher accuracy than state-of-the-art methods listed in Table 4 (e.g., Krizhevsky et al. AlexNet 65%, He et al. ResNet-101 71%).

ISIC-2018.notes: Class weights were calculated from support samples to average the metrics. Comparison with reference [56] (PNASNet-5-Large) showed superior performance. Reference [56] results pertained to the validation set (193 samples), while this study's results are on the test set (1512 samples).

ISIC-2018 (Alternative Split for Compari.split: 30% reserved for testing (3005 images), 70% for training/validation (7010 images)
ISIC-2018 (Alternative Split for Compari.notes: This split was used to ensure a fair comparison with reference [39], which did not utilize the standard test data. Among the proposed methods, the modified EfficientNet-B4 showed the best performance in this setting.

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble of Modified Inception ResNet v2 and Modified EfficientNet-B4
architecture_used.pretraining: Inception ResNet v2 was pre-trained on ImageNet database; EfficientNet-B4 details not explicitly specified but implied transfer learning context
architecture_used.input_size: 299 × 299 pixels for Inception ResNet v2; 380 × 380 pixels for EfficientNet-B4

modifications: Integration of Soft-Attention (SA) mechanism in the Inception ResNet C block
Use of Inception ResNet B block for feature extraction
Max pool layer following the blocks
Concatenation layer of filters corresponding to the inception block
0.5 dropout layer after activation function to regularize output
modified_inception_resnet_v2.feature_extraction: Merging features of two layers of Inception ResNet v2

modifications: Addition of two dense layers after the flatten layer

architecture_details.ensemble_method: Soft voting strategy to aggregate predictions from both models

data_augmentation: Nearest neighbor interpolation for pixel interpolation

classification_or_output_head.description: Aggregates probability distributions from the modified Inception ResNet v2 and modified EfficientNet-B4 for final classification

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Triplet loss function with Inception ResNet v2
Directed acyclic graph with binary CNNs
preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation: Random rotation (up to 180 degrees), horizontal and vertical shifting (up to 10%), zooming (up to 10%), and horizontal and vertical flipping.
Interpolation: Nearest neighbor interpolation applied to pixels in transformed images.
Normalization: Pixel values scaled to the range [-1, 1].
Resizing: Images resized to 299 × 299 pixels for the ISIC-2017 dataset and 380 × 380 pixels for the ISIC-2018 dataset.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Random rotation of images by up to 180 degrees.
Horizontal shifting of images by up to 10% of their width.
Vertical shifting of images by up to 10% of their height.
Zooming of images by up to 10%.
Nearest neighbor interpolation applied to pixels in the transformed images.
Pixel values scaled to the range [-1, 1].

conclusions: The proposed ensemble method, combining a modified Inception ResNet v2 with a Soft-Attention mechanism and a modified EfficientNet-B4 with added dense layers, achieves higher accuracy than current state-of-the-art methods on the ISIC-2017 and ISIC-2018 datasets.

The Soft-Attention mechanism effectively enhances network performance by highlighting informative features and suppressing noise-inducing ones, addressing challenges posed by the variability in skin lesion types, textures, colors, and shapes.
The ensemble approach using soft voting leverages the complementary strengths of the two base models, resulting in more robust and accurate classification compared to standalone models.
On the ISIC-2017 dataset, the ensemble method achieved a weighted average accuracy of 79.5%, outperforming existing techniques such as AlexNet (65%) and ResNet-101 (71%).
On the ISIC-2018 dataset, the ensemble method achieved a weighted average accuracy of 85.18%, demonstrating superior performance compared to reference methods like PNASNet-5-Large.

When compared to Huo et al. [39] on a specific 30% test split of the ISIC-2018 dataset, the proposed ensemble method achieved an accuracy of 88.21% and an AUC of 0.9818, outperforming the reference method's accuracy of 85.85% and AUC of 0.9585.
Among the individual models tested, the modified EfficientNet-B4 showed the best performance in the specific comparison setting with Huo et al. [39], achieving 89.15% accuracy.
The study acknowledges that adjusting hyper-parameters may lead to better recognition rates in future work.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on public datasets (ISIC-2017 and ISIC-2018) without external validation on independent clinical cohorts, limiting generalizability to real-world clinical settings.

Class imbalance is significant, particularly in ISIC-2018 where classes like Dermatofibroma (44 test samples) and Vascular lesions (35 test samples) have very low support, potentially skewing performance metrics.

The comparison with Huo et al. [39] utilized a non-standard 70/30 train/test split of the ISIC-2018 dataset rather than the official test set, which may not be directly comparable to other studies using the standard split.

The comparison with reference [56] (PNASNet-5-Large) is flawed because the reference results were reported on the validation set (193 samples), while the proposed method's results were on the test set (1512 samples).
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed to determine the statistical significance of the performance improvements over state-of-the-art methods.

The paper acknowledges that hyperparameter tuning was not fully optimized and suggests that adjusting hyperparameters could lead to better recognition rates, indicating potential for further improvement.

The model was trained on approximately 12,000 images for ISIC-2017 and 29,000 for ISIC-2018, but the text notes that combining large databases with CNNs can lead to overfitting if training instances per class are small, though data augmentation was used to mitigate this.

The study does not report results on binary melanoma vs. non-melanoma classification separately, focusing instead on multi-class classification which may mask performance issues on the critical melanoma class.
